speaker: interviewer
text: --25, 1996. OK. We were just talking about exit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Exit. Trying to get out of Germany and preparing ourselves now, having a goal, having permission to go to Shanghai, China, the part that was occupied by the Japanese forces. This is a strange thing all by itself. The Japanese were allies of the Germans, and yet there were some very sympathetic Japanese counsels that stamped entrance visas to Shanghai by the hundreds, by the thousands, to get the Jews out of Germany. There are several theories of why that happened. But be it as it may, between 18,000 and 20,000 Jewish refugees, mostly from Germany, Austria, Poland, and some Czechoslovakians, I think, then went to China-- Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Try to get out of Berlin. How do you take everything in your house that you have accumulated for the last 20, 30 years, pack it up, and go to China? Well, it's impossible. The first thing was that you were not allowed to take everything out of Germany. So the first thing that you had to do, that the German government made you do, they made you make lists of every item, I mean, every item in your house-- every needle, every thread, every button, every spoon, every dish. Everything had to be listed. I think that we might still even have these lists, that thick. You had to submit those to the Berlin government. How did they know if you put everything on or if you didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't answer that. You were supposed to put everything on-- every picture, everything. Then the lists came back redlined. This is what you are not allowed to take out. Included, of course, in that was art objects, anything that had any gold or silver around it, any crystal, any art object. But that I know for sure. What else wasn't allowed, I-- I don't remember. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember packing your own belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's-- let's-- let's do it this way. All right, so now the list came back. And now you're ready to pack. OK? You hire, like, the Mayflower people, you know, and they come. And apparently at that time, you put all your belongings in two distinctly different categories. One of them were [? lifts, ?] wooden crates. And the others were large stevedore suitcases, trunks. Big ones. I don't know what the distinction is. And I think what went into the wooden crates and what went into the-- the other suitcases was helter-skelter. One guy from the Berlin government came to oversee the packing. What went into those, what was redlined, you couldn't take out. You were just supposed to leave it there for the government to confiscate. And that's what they did. They confiscated part of your belongs. I remember my father taking this man with the lists, sitting him down at a table, a bottle of cognac or whiskey next to him, and a glass. After the first hour, he didn't know what went into those boxes. The people that packed the suitcases were also sympathetic to our cause. I might mention at that time-- that at this time, that not everybody was a Nazi. There were very, very many Germans that were sympathetic towards the Jews, tried to help them in any way they could without endangering their lives too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those were some of the people that said, it's OK. Put it in there. Put it in there. And so consequently, quite a few of the contraband, if that's what you want to call it, went into both the suitcases, the trunks, and the wooden crates.

speaker: interviewee
text: What we didn't know at that time was that-- I don't know who was in the know. I don't know if the-- the people that packed the suitcases knew it. I don't think so. What happened at that time is that the German government had no intentions whatsoever to let the wooden crates out of the country. When they left our house, were loaded onto the trucks. They were unloaded someplace else. All the trunks were put on the train ahead of us. All the wooden crates were put in a warehouse in Hamburg, where I understand consequently, during the war, were bombed and burned. But there never was any intention of letting any of that stuff get out of Germany. So what we were left with when we finally ended up in Shanghai was none of the wooden crates but all of our trunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is jumping way ahead, but did you ever-- when you made your trip back to Germany, did you ever retrieve anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No, no, no, no. No, no. For one thing, like I said, I never went to Hamburg. And we-- we had-- this was research, by the way. After the war was over, we got some-- we-- we claimed some of our losses, and we got some money back. Educational losses, monetary losses, at a very small percentage. But yes, there were some of it. And that's how we found out that those crates were not lost. They were deliberately diverted to Hamburg, and according to what they said, were consequently bombed and then destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let me ask you again now, do you remember specifically anything that-- a personal belonging that was important to you that you packed? Were you able to get anything that meant something to you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. Not really. I was always very interested in trains. I had a train set in Berlin. It was Marklin. It was small. It was HO. And my father and I were very active in the modeling and the building at-- of a layout.

speaker: interviewer
text: And matter of fact, that layout, roughly, maybe four, by eight feet in size, was situated in the operatory in my father's dental-- dental room, where he had one corner of it. And one of my uncle-- uncles, who was very good in painting scenery, painted a scenery all around it on paper. Do you know that I still have that? I still have that background. It's in my garage here in Denver.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, a patient came, saw that. He was a photographer. He took some pictures of it. The apartments had very high ceilings in Germany, 10 to 15 feet high. And we had a ladder. He climbed on that ladder and took some aerial pictures of that. We sent those pictures to some friends of ours outside the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were confiscated, and my father got in trouble. He says, what are you doing taking pictures of railroads in Germany? It looked so real. They came to our house, and matter of fact, I think they were going to arrest my father. They had the pictures, and my father said, yeah, here, there. It's a layout. It's a model train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well. They left with egg on their face, you know. It was just one of the incidents. And-- so anyway, part of that train, all of that train, was packed, unfortunately, in the wooden crates. And in Shanghai, there were some last-minute purchases that my father apparently made. They were, like, two or three locomotives. There was a-- a number of cars. There was some accessories that we ended up with in Shanghai, which-- in Shanghai, where things became tough-- you kind of lived by selling your goods. And I sold those Marklin trains to a very prosperous Chinese, who then invited me to come to his house to set up the train set. And I went there, I think, once or twice to-- to help him build the train set.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I jump back one step? Before you got to Shanghai, you said that not everybody was-- was mean and everything. Do you have any recollections of anyone that was particularly good or-- or bad to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember two instances especially. One of them was, like everything else, Germany was rationed. The Jewish rations were maybe a third of what everybody else got. There was always, when we went to the grocery stores, some extra food that we could get kind of under the table. I remember one time we got our weekly meat rations from the butcher shop. In those days, they didn't have a big Safeway store. You went to the butcher shop, you went to the milk shop, you went to the-- you know, everything was separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- for some reason or another, all of the meat disappeared. Stolen, lost, I don't know what happened to it. We went back to the butcher shop and asked the butcher, did we by any chance leave it here? No, you didn't. Oh, my goodness. Now we have nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was the lady of the owner says, Mrs. Chaim, you come after hours. We'll have something for you. My mother went back after hours and got the meat, the rations, again. One other incident happened in about 1934 or '35-- I don't really know when-- when we lived at the Burgenstrasse 10A. We walked up the stairs, and there were two opposing entrances to two apartments, one to the right, one to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day our neighbor-- the other door-- met my father on the platform there. And he said, Dr. Chaim, this is the last time that I can acknowledge you. This is the last time that I can say good morning, hello, how are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will endanger the life of myself and my family if I acknowledge you as a living being. I'm sorry, but this is the way it has to be. You know, he-- he wasn't exactly a friend of ours, but he wasn't a Nazi either. And then, of course, the biggest thing that happened to us that saved our lives was the fact that when all the permissions were granted for us to leave Berlin, on a Friday evening, a train that went especially from Berlin through Russia into Manchuria and then by boat to China-- we were pegged to be arrested. The week before, apparently a friend of my father who was either a policeman or a detective who had access to the lists of people to be arrested found my father's name on that list, came to our house at night in the dark, and warned my father, you cannot leave on Friday. They'll arrest you. Maybe on Thursday. You-- the latest date is Tuesday. You've got to get out before that. He took-- he took his life in his hands to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I do not know who he was. I was never able to find out his name, anything. That's lost. I do know that in 1943, as late as that, he got found, he got caught, put against the wall, and shot as a traitor. And that was the thing. You help a Jew, you lose your life. There was no other choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you get out on that Tuesday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. Helter-skelter, everything at the last moment, tried to do whatever you had to do, sell some of the furniture, this, that, and the other. We on Tuesday tried to leave to go to the station. How do you get to the station on time? Jews were not allowed to be on the street at night. Jews were not allowed to hire a taxi. My father had a very good friend, Oskar Finger, who lived around the corner from us, three blocks away, on [GERMAN]. I don't know how I remember all these facts, but he did. He was also a dentist. His wife was his assistant, and they were good friends of ours. His wife had blond hair, blue eyes, didn't look Jewish at all. They in turn had a friend who was a taxi driver. And the taxi driver told Ellie Finger, Oskar Finger's wife, if you ever need a cab, call me. I will help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Ellie Finger called this man and said, I have some friends of ours who need to leave tonight. They need to get to Bahnhof Zoo. This is where you're supposed to go. What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bahnhof Zoo was the station where the train left from. This was a transport, a train where a lot of Jews were on. And he says, I will do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a ticket on this train already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh yes, we had a ticket on the train. So he came. We took our little bags, our bread-- we had some provisions-- our blankets. We had some blankets. And he said, Dr. Chaim, I will not take you to Bahnhof Zoo. It is too dangerous to go to Bahnhof Zoo, and besides, I can't make it anyway. I have been there before. I know what it is like. It is pandemonium. Trust me. I will take you to Leiter Bahnhof. There will be nobody there, but the train will stop. I know the station agent. Trust me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could we do? At that time we still had quite a bit of German money, which was worth absolutely nothing when you get out of the border or the country. What was the date now? Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I do not know the date when we left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was October, October 1940. And as I understand it, it was-- there was only one more transport, one more filled with Jewish refugees, that left Berlin to go through Russia into China.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Anhalter Bahnof. He talked to the station master. The station master said, I will take care of it. My father again peeled away some 100 mark [GERMAN]-- you know, a lot of-- we had-- at that time we had a lot of money. Gave it to the station master.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train came. The train stopped. He went on the train. Found a compartment in that train where there were some German soldiers, Wehrmacht, just soldiers smoking in a nonsmoking compartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: The station master, the conductor. That was the conductor of the train now. And he said to these people, he says, gentlemen, you are not allowed to smoke in a nonsmoking compartment. You have to leave. I will find you a better compartment in the smoking area. This is Hitler Germany. They threw out the German Army people and put in us into this compartment. We left on the train on time, and we were on the train. This is roughly 8, 9 o'clock at night. We left Berlin, being scared, of course, will somebody notify the police, the SS, the Gestapo-- who knows-- the Chaims have left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had your exit visas now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, yeah, yeah. We had-- we had all-- everything. You see, this was the thing that the Nazis did. Why should we bother packing everything up? Let them pack it up. And then we just walk in, and everything's already nice and packed up, see. So of course there was fear. I was not feeling good at all because the day before, we got the last of the typhus shot, and I had a rather high fever. And I remember that afternoon laying on the couch, maybe even a little bit delirious. And I also remember on the train laying down, kind of not knowing what the heck's going on. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 12, 12 years old at that time. We're on the train. The train is leaving Berlin. The train is going towards, I believe, Russia through Poland. We get to the suburbs of Kustrin, and they stop the train in the middle of the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kustrin is a town which might have already been in Poland. I don't know. The reason they stopped the train is because the English Air Force is bombing the town. If they would have hit the tracks, if they would have hit the station, if they would have delayed the train, the order that Dr. Chaim is fleeing the country might have reached us. As it was, they did not hit the tracks. They did not hit the station. And after a while, the train moved again, and we kept going.

speaker: interviewer
text: We got to the border between Germany and Russia-- I don't know where that was-- in the morning. I remember it was daylight. Now this is it. Is there-- are-- are there orders to arrest Dr. Chaim, Mrs. Chaim, the children? This is-- now comes fingernail-biting time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The custom at that time was, by the Germans, to fleece the people for contraband. And it was done in various ways. Strip yourself naked, open your luggage, search the compartments, in-- in various orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in our case, nothing. Nothing. Hardly at-- they did, I think, check my father's wallet. And my father said, all I have here is some German marks. Hidden on the inside were a few US dollars. My father-- my father was something else again. He was quite an operator. And-- but luckily, I don't think-- I remember nothing was ever searched that particular time. Maybe they were in a hurry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At that time, there was no name, no anything. My father went on the platform, talked to a baggage handler, a redcap, and said, would you please be so kind and send a telegram to this-and-this address? And he gave him a piece of paper and a lot of money, the last of our money, and says, if you see the last car of this train go across the border with any of my family-- here we are, look at us-- being dragged off at the last minute, could you send this telegram to this address?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I'll do it. The telegram said, 'The flowers have arrived safely.' The telegram was sent to my grandmother. My grandmother was ill. She did not have any permission to leave Germany. She was not-- unable anyway. She was too sick. She was in a home, in a nursing home. My grandmother got the telegram. She went to bed that night with the telegram in her hand and did not wake up the next day. Now, if she-- she was old and feeble anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she help it along? I don't know. Did she commit suicide? I don't know. But definitely she was old, she was sick, she was abandoned, left in Hitler Germany. So we found that out three months later when the relatives of my father, the Dessens, got a letter through various ways informing them that my grandmother passed away. They didn't want to tell my mother because my mother still did not get over the fact that oh, boy, we are in Shanghai. And this is her mother that passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's her mother that passed away. So my father knew it, didn't want to tell my mother. A day or two later, my mother related a dream to my father. I dreamt of an open grave. I think my mother is dead. And that's when my father-- mental telepathy?-- and that's when my father told her, yes, she-- she passed away. So those are just-- That's how we got out of Germany. Now we're on the train. The first thing that happen after you go into Russia, being a train fan, is the fact that the train tracks are not compatible with the Russian tracks. The whole world has 4 foot 8 and 1/2 inches between the two tracks. Russia has 5 foot 2 inches between the tracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they take those trains, they jack them up, take the tracks out, and put the Russian tracks under. We continued to the first town, which was Minsk, which had a large Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews came to the train, and we were starving. We were hungry, shall we say. Fed us some of this food that we haven't had in years. We all got sick. [LAUGHS] We couldn't take that kind of food. My father was in the army in the first World War. And he was actually at that time guarding the Russian soldiers in Minsk, that was occupied at that time by the-- by the Germans. He wanted to see what the town looked like. We weren't allowed to leave the train station. He did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't mean to interrupt you, but let's change the tape here so we can continue on the next one.
